Which EMDE countries excel in green innovation?

Ralf Martin

02 July 2026

Introduction

Development and the green transition need to go hand in hand. Many of the most dramatic development and growth success stories are built around waves of new technology and innovation such as electronics, wireless and ICT technologies for countries such as Japan, Korea, Taiwan and China. Of course, China has also led the way in using the Green Technology Wave as a way for development. Indeed, there is concern that China could be so far advanced in solar or battery technology that not even high-income countries are able to catch up - and that this could impose limitations on sovereignty.

However, a challenge of the clean transition is that greenhouse gases are connected to so many human activities. What’s more, we are increasingly facing the challenge of having to adapt to climate change.

As a consequence we need to push the innovation frontier in many different technology areas. Thus, there is likely a lot of potential for growth success stories to arise. However, not all countries are equally well placed and not all countries can succeed in the same technologies.

In this dashboard we examine which countries show potential based on their existing innovation landscape as revealed via global patent data. Throughout we assign (patented) innovation to countries on the basis of the location of the associated innovators and the location of owner (assignee) of the underlying patent.

The global green technology innovation landscape

Panel (a) of Figure 1 illustrates that only a very small share (less than 1%) of global green technology innovation originates from innovators based in EMDE once we exclude China, whereas China alone accounts for more than two thirds of the group × family observations in this space. Of course, small EMDE shares are a feature of all types of innovation. But unlike in some other technology areas, EMDE (ex China) are also less specialised in green technology than the global average — their revealed technological advantage (the symmetric Balassa index) is 0.71, whereas China is the only group above parity (1.04) and HICs sit just below it (0.93).

In panel (b) of Figure 1 we examine the average citation count - a common quality indicator of innovations - of green technology innovation compared to average citation counts for all innovations for various country groupings. In contrast to the volume picture of panel (a), this suggests no quality penalty for green innovation: in every country group, green technologies are cited more than the group’s average innovation. For EMDE (ex China) the premium is particularly pronounced - green innovations receive 2.51 citations on average against 1.86 for all EMDE innovations. A quality gap relative to HICs remains (HIC green innovations receive 3.14 citations), but EMDE green innovation is clearly not a low-quality niche within EMDE innovation. Citations are also an indicator for knowledge spillovers, and increasing R&D investments in areas with particularly high knowledge spillovers can be a growth enhancing strategy. However, citation counts ignore a number of considerations. Firstly, simple citation counts ignore that the value of knowledge spillovers can vary substantially, both because the economic value of citing innovations varies but also because citation counts ignore indirect effects. Secondly, citation counts ignore that the cost of generating innovations - and thus spillovers - varies potentially between technology types. Thirdly, if we are concerned about the growth of EMDE in particular, we need to take into account the location of the recipients of spillovers: what matters then is not the average spillover but the spillover flows that land within EMDE.

Panels (c) and (d) turn from citations to the monetary value of knowledge spillovers and to the returns from further R&D investment.1 Panel (c) shows the average value of global spillovers (in $ million). As with citations, HIC innovations generate on average the most valuable spillovers, but green innovations generate above-average spillovers within every group - within EMDE, $7.4 million against $5.1 million for the average EMDE innovation. Panel (d) shows global returns to additional R&D investment: green innovation in EMDE (86.2%) far outstrips the EMDE all-technology average (50.0%) and approaches HIC green innovation (105.9%), while in China green returns sit below the country’s all-technology average. Returns from spillovers that benefit EMDE innovators specifically are examined at the country level in Figure 4; on average, too, they favour green innovation in EMDE (6.05%, against 4.60% for all EMDE innovations and 2.59% for HIC green innovation). Indeed, for a policy maker who cares about growth in EMDE, providing public funds to enhance such investments could be rationalised by this result.

Figure 1: Green technology innovation — counts, citations, value and returns by income group (2013-2022)

Note. All four panels attribute innovations to income groups by inventor location, with one observation per family within each group: a family with inventors in several countries of the same group counts once for that group, one spanning groups once per group. (a) the number of green-technology innovations by group (total 2,265,839 observations). (b) mean forward citations per family, green technology vs all innovations. (c) the mean value of global spillovers (expected global spillover value, $ million). (d) mean global returns from additional R&D — the returns-to-R&D index (expected spillover value scaled by R&D cost and patent survival), computed over innovations with an estimated positive R&D cost and expressed as a percentage (×100). The dotted horizontal line in panels (b)-(d) marks the height of the HIC “All innovations” bar — the HIC all-technology average — as a common reference. Error bars in (b)-(d) are 95% confidence intervals for the mean (mean ± 1.96·SE); with very large samples per group they are mostly narrower than the bars.

Figure 2 shows how these volumes evolved over the 2013-2022 sample. HIC output was roughly flat at 60-70 thousand green innovations per year. China’s, by contrast, exploded: from under 6,000 in 2013 — less than a tenth of the HIC volume — it overtook the HICs in 2016 and reached several hundred thousand innovations a year by the early 2020s. Green innovation in EMDE (ex China) was essentially flat throughout, at roughly 2,000-2,400 families a year (note the log scale). The dips at the right edge of the figure reflect publication and processing lags in the patent data rather than real declines.

Figure 2: Green technology innovations per year by income group

Note. Number of green-technology innovations per family year (the family’s earliest filing year, from the LMICinnovation fam_year map) for each income group, 2013-2022. Same attribution as Figure 1a: one observation per country group × family. The y-axis is logarithmic — China’s rise spans two orders of magnitude while EMDE (ex China) is roughly flat. Counts in the last two years are depressed by publication and processing lags (patent families appear in the data with delay), so the right-edge declines should not be read as real drops in innovation activity.

Figure 2b repeats the exercise for all innovations. The pattern closely mirrors the green one: Chinese patenting overtakes the HICs in 2016 and climbs to roughly 2.4 million families a year, HIC output stays flat at around 700 thousand, and EMDE (ex China) is flat at 30-40 thousand. China’s green surge is thus part of a much broader patenting boom rather than a green-specific push — consistent with its green RTA of just above parity in the text above.

Figure 2b: All innovations per year by income group

Note. As Figure 2, but counting all innovations in the patent universe rather than green technology only: one observation per country group × family, by the family’s earliest filing year (2013-2022), on a logarithmic y-axis. The right-edge years are again affected by publication and processing lags.

Dividing the two series gives Figure 2c: the share of green technology in each group’s innovation output. Through most of the sample the ranking is stable — HICs devote roughly 9-10% of their innovation to green technology, EMDE (ex China) about 6-8%, and China around 8%. From 2021 China’s green share climbs steeply, to about 12% in 2021 and 26% in 2022; as before, the right-edge years should be read with caution, since publication lags can affect the green tagging and the overall counts differently.

Figure 2c: Share of green technology in each group’s innovations

Note. The share of green technology in each group’s annual innovation output: the Figure 2 counts divided by the Figure 2b counts, per family year (2013-2022) and country group (one observation per group × family in both numerator and denominator; linear scale). Right-edge years inherit the publication-lag caveats of Figures 2-2b, which can affect the green tagging and the overall counts differently.

Heterogeneity across countries

That said, the country groupings combine a range of highly diverse countries. In Figure 4 we explore this heterogeneity with scatter plots that compare each country’s green innovation against two benchmarks. The vertical axis plots the country’s green average relative to the global green average; the horizontal axis plots the country’s green average relative to its own average across all technologies. In other words, the vertical axis tells us whether it makes sense to invest in a particular country conditional on wanting to invest in green technology; the horizontal axis tells us whether it makes sense to focus on green technology conditional on focusing on a particular country. Panel (a) does this in terms of the average value of global spillovers per innovation (as in Figure 1c); panel (b) in terms of the average global returns to additional R&D (as in Figure 1d). In panel (b) a number of developing countries are above one (i.e. above average) on both dimensions, and a fairly large number more are above average in terms of local options (horizontal axis). In the following we distinguish between four country groupings: high-income countries (HIC); China (kept separate); and two groups of developing economies (LMIC excl. China) — the HighX2 group (high on both indicators), comprising the developing countries where both panel-(b) indicators exceed 1: India, Turkey, Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam, Iran, Serbia and Indonesia (the orange developing countries in panel b); and the remaining developing countries, labelled nonHighX2. Roughly half of developing green innovation sits in each group (donut c). We use the labels HighX2 and nonHighX2 consistently in the figures and text that follow.

Figure 4: Green gap scatter — average global spillover value and returns to R&D

Note. Each bubble is a country with at least 50 green-technology patent families, sized by log10(number of green families). In both panels x = the green gap (the country’s green average ÷ its own all-technology average) and y = the country’s green average relative to the global green average (dashed lines mark parity). Panel (a) uses the average global spillover value per innovation (expected global spillover value, as in Figure 1c); panel (b) the average global returns from additional R&D (the returns-to-R&D index, as in Figure 1d). Both panels are coloured by panel (b)’s classification, which defines the grouping used in the remainder of the report: HighX2 = developing countries with both panel-(b) indicators above 1, nonHighX2 = the remaining developing countries. The donut (c) shows, among developing (LMIC ex-China) countries, how their green-technology families split between HighX2 (orange) and nonHighX2 (purple) — shares of summed green families.

Heterogeneity across technologies

Green technology also comprises a wide range of different sub-technologies. We explore this in Figure 5, showing the distribution across the nine green technology classes. Green Energy is the largest class for every country group, accounting for between 28% and 38% of innovations. Beyond that, however, there is clear variation across country groups. HICs have a distinct focus on Green Transport (close to 20% of their green innovations, roughly twice the share of the developing groups). China leans towards Adaptation and Green Manufacturing. Among the developing groups, the HighX2 group stands out for Green ICT (16% of its green innovations, far more than any other group) and Green Transport, whereas the nonHighX2 group tilts towards Adaptation (23%) and Circular Economy technologies (the highest shares of any group).

Figure 5: Green tech subsector mix by country group

Note. For each country group from Figure 4, the distribution of its green-technology innovations across the nine green-technology subsectors. An innovation (patent family) is attributed to a group if it has an inventor in a country of that group, deduplicated within the group; shares are subsector counts divided by the group’s total subsector tags, so they sum to 100% within each group (a family tagged with several subsectors contributes to each). Only countries with at least 50 green-technology families (those classified in Figure 4) are included.

The sections on innovation clusters and innovating firms & institutions (Figures 6-11) are still being computed and will appear here in a subsequent update.

Collaboration in green-technology innovation

The prominent presence of MNEs in EMDE green technology innovation might lead to at least two concerns. Firstly, could this be symptomatic of a diversion of intellectual resources in ways that are not conducive to developing-country outcomes? Secondly - and to the contrary - could the presence of MNEs in developing countries imply a trade-off where such ventures by MNEs benefit developing countries while compromising outcomes elsewhere? In this section we explore this by looking at the relative quality of innovations by collaboration type between HIC and EMDE institutions and inventors.

How prevalent are these collaborations? From the perspective of HICs they are tiny. About 1% of HIC green innovations are with EMDE other than China (Figure 12a). This contrasts with 9% of innovations that involve collaborations with Chinese inventors and firms and 11% with inventors in other HICs. For Chinese innovations the share of international collaboration of any kind is even smaller, at around 4%. By contrast, for EMDE excluding China, collaborations with HICs are an important category: for the HighX2 group these amount to nearly two thirds of its green innovations (54% with non-China EMDE-HIC teams plus 10% that also involve China), while for the nonHighX2 group they account for roughly a fifth (19% plus 3%), with over three quarters of its innovation purely national. We also find that international collaboration with non-HIC-based inventors and firms is rather a rarity (2% or less in every group).

Figure 12: How collaborative is innovation, by group

Note. Share of each group’s green-technology innovations by inventor-team type (National only, and the four international types by partner income mix — with the North–South mix split into HIC × China and HIC × non-China EMDE). A family is attributed to a group if it has an inventor in one of that group’s countries (Figure 4 classification), so a mixed family appears in several groups; inventor countries come from the geocoded countrymap (full coverage, as in the LMICinnovation analysis). For the HIC group this separates its developing-world collaboration into the part running through China (red) versus the rest of the developing world (orange). Some types are structurally absent for a group: the HIC group has no non-HIC only teams, and the China / developing groups have no HIC only (or, for China, no HIC × non-China EMDE) teams. (Green here is the iseapp “Green Technology” tech group.)

The importance of HIC-EMDE collaborations for EMDE becomes even more poignant when looking at marginal returns in Figure 13. Note that returns from collaborations mixing HIC and developing-country inventors are consistently high across all four country groups, ranging from roughly 84% to 181% in global terms. Notably, this includes HIC, where returns from collaborations with China (140%) or with other EMDE (120%) are at least as high as returns from collaborations with other HICs (121%). In the nonHighX2 group, HIC collaboration returns (84%) are three times as high as for non-collaborative innovation (28%), and in China the pattern is similar (140% vs 61%). In the HighX2 group the collaboration premium is smaller (129% vs 99%) — purely national innovation there is already of high quality — while its teams combining HIC and Chinese inventors reach the highest returns of all (181%).

How does this help to answer the two questions we posed at the beginning of this section? Does HIC collaboration divert the focus of EMDE innovators towards HIC benefits? Perhaps partially: in the HighX2 group the return gap between HIC collaboration and national-only innovation accounting for global innovation spillovers (129.4%/99.5%=1.3) is somewhat larger than when accounting only for EMDE spillovers: 8.26/7.49=1.1. However, it remains the case that HIC collaboration generates the highest returns. Hence, any possible pro-HIC bias is more than compensated for by the HIC quality effect.

Secondly, is there evidence that EMDE collaboration - while beneficial for EMDE - compromises the quality of innovation at the global level? We suggest that there should only be such a concern if we were to find that global returns for collaborative innovations are vastly lower than for purely national or HIC-HIC collaborations. As suggested earlier, we find the opposite.

Figure 13: Innovation quality by collaboration type for Green Technology innovation

Note. Mean global returns to additional R&D for green-technology innovation by group and inventor-team type (×100, %). Error bars are 95% confidence intervals for the mean (mean ± 1.96·SE, lower bound clipped at 0). Same family attribution and team types as Figure 12 (the North–South mix split into HIC × China and HIC × non-China EMDE). Across all groups, internationally-collaborative innovations — especially mixed HIC × developing teams — carry higher returns than national-only innovations, most starkly for the nonHighX2 group and China; in the HighX2 group purely national innovation is already close behind (the wider intervals for the developing groups reflect their smaller samples).

Conclusion

Is it a good idea for EMDE to engage in green technology innovation? In terms of volume, EMDE (excluding China) today account for less than 1% of global green innovation and - unlike in some other technology areas - are less specialised in it than either HICs or China. The quality picture, however, is considerably brighter: green innovations are cited above the all-technology average in every country group, and the global returns to additional green R&D in EMDE far exceed the EMDE all-technology average (on average 86% vs 50%). The case becomes strongest once we focus on knowledge spillovers whose beneficiaries are in developing countries only: green innovation in EMDE then carries higher returns than green innovation in HICs or China and beats the average of other technologies within EMDE (on average 6.05% vs 4.60%). Average results hide considerable heterogeneity between countries and detailed technologies. We identify a set of EMDE — the HighX2 group — where returns to green R&D are both higher than for alternative technologies within the country and above the global green average. The five largest countries in terms of green innovation counts in this category are India, Turkey, Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam. We also highlight where these countries’ technological strengths lie: Green Energy dominates everywhere, but the HighX2 group tilts relatively towards Green ICT and Green Transport technologies. Moreover, we examine which kinds of firms or research institutes are driving promising innovation. This reveals an outsized importance of multinational firms often headquartered in HICs but with research labs or collaborating innovators in EMDE. It is those firms and collaborations that contribute the innovations with the highest internal and external returns, both from a global and an EMDE perspective. Hence, encouraging further engagement by such firms in diversified R&D activities could be a channel to increase innovation quality, contribute to economic growth and mobilise private funding for innovation in EMDE.

Appendix: How the value, cost and spillover-return indicators are built

The value and return indicators used throughout this note — the expected global spillover value, the expected spillover value to EMDE (excl. China), and the returns to additional R&D — are produced by the Patent Rank methodology of Guillard et al. (2021), applied to the 2025 PATSTAT patent universe. The same construction underlies the green-technology analysis of Martin & Verhoeven (2023) and the IFC (2025) green-technology report (the methodology corresponds to Box 3.1 and Appendix 2 of that report; see also CEP Special Paper 54). The patent-level private value is from Kogan et al. (2017). This appendix sketches the various steps:

  1. Inferring the private values of innovation from event studies and interpolating them to non-stock listed firms
  2. Computing a centrality measure with an economic interpretation by combining the PageRank approach with individual private values obtained in step 1, which we call Patent Rank
  3. We use the distribution of private value to identify the parameters of a simple model of the innovation process, with parameters \(K_c\) and \(\alpha_c\) capturing the cost of innovation steps and the skewness of the idea-generation function in technology area c
  4. We combine 2 and 3 to compute estimates of the marginal return of further R&D in different technology and country cells.

Private patent value

Every patent \(i\) carries a private value \(v_i\) (USD millions) following Kogan et al. (2017): for patents granted to publicly listed firms, \(v_i\) is read off the abnormal stock-market return of the patentee in a narrow window around the grant date — the market’s estimate of the present value of the cash flows attributable to that single patent, net of the firm-level common factor. For patents whose owners are not listed, \(v_i\) is imputed from observable features (4-digit CPC class, claim count, patent-family size, …) estimated on the listed-firm subset, giving a \(v_i\) for every patent in the universe.

Patent Rank

Raw forward-citation counts treat a citation from a low-value patent the same as one from a high-value patent and ignore chains of citations across generations. Patent Rank corrects both by adapting the PageRank algorithm to the patent citation network. The rank \(PR_i\) solves the linear system

\[ PR_i \;=\; v_i \;+\; d \sum_{j \in F(i)} w_{ji}\,PR_j , \]

where \(F(i)\) is the set of patents that directly cite \(i\); \(w_{ji} = 1/n_j\), with \(n_j\) the number of backward citations of the citing patent \(j\) (each citer spreads its rank uniformly over what it builds on); and \(d \in (0,1)\) is a damping factor, set to \(d = 0.5\). Solved iteratively across the millions of patents in the network, \(PR_i\) equals the inventor’s own private value \(v_i\) plus a discounted share of the private values of every patent that builds on \(i\) directly or indirectly — i.e. the total social value that patent \(i\) ultimately seeds along the subsequent innovation chain.

Spillover flows by group - To isolate the social value that reaches a given inventor-country group \(G\), the same system is re-solved with \(v_j = 0\) for every patent \(j\) whose inventors are outside \(G\). The group-restricted rank \(PR_i^{G}\) then measures only the spillovers from \(i\) that land on group-\(G\) inventors. Averaging per innovation gives this note’s value indicators: the expected global spillover value keeps every downstream patent; the expected spillover value to EMDE (excl. China) keeps only the EMDE-ex-China downstream patents (HIC and other group-to-group flows are constructed the same way).

A simple model of the innovation process

Patent values vary not only because some ideas are better, but because technology fields differ in how expensive a patentable innovation is and how skewed the quality of ideas is. A simple structural model fitted at the 4-digit CPC level separates the two.

  • Model. In field \(c\), ideas are drawn from a skewed (Pareto) quality distribution with shape parameter \(\alpha_c\) — a larger \(\alpha_c\) means more mass near zero (fewer “superstar” ideas, a thinner right tail). Turning an idea into a patentable innovation requires a sunk R&D cost \(K_c\), paid before the realised value is known.
  • The kink. If values were known in advance, only ideas worth more than \(K_c\) would ever be developed, and the value distribution would be sharply truncated at \(K_c\). Because outcomes are uncertain, some ex-post low-value innovations get developed anyway. The combination of skewed idea generation and a sunk-cost cutoff produces a characteristic kink in the empirical patent-value distribution.
  • Identification. Fitting the model to the \(v_i\) distribution within each field recovers both parameters from two distinct features of the data: the location of the kink identifies \(K_c\) (the further right the kink, the higher the cost), and the curvature of the distribution to the right of the kink identifies \(\alpha_c\). Estimates differ sharply across fields — e.g. organic fine chemistry has \(K_c \approx \$25\)M per innovation with a relatively thick right tail, whereas semiconductors have \(K_c \approx \$4.4\)M with very few innovations valued above $60M.

From private value to social returns and spillover flows

The fitted model and the resulting parameters, combined with the Patent-Rank construction, yield a marginal-social-return index. At the field level it is \(\mathrm{ISTRAX}_c = (\alpha_c / K_c)\,\overline{SR}_c\), where \(\overline{SR}_c\) is the average social component of Patent Rank (\(PR_i - v_i\), the spillover left once the inventor’s own appropriation is netted out) across innovations in field \(c\). A smaller cost \(K_c\) (each marginal R&D dollar buys more projects) and a larger \(\alpha_c\) (more idea mass ready to develop at the margin) both raise the return. In this note we use the patent-level counterpart, averaged within each group,

\[ \text{return}_i \;=\; \frac{\alpha_c + 1}{K_c}\,\cdot\, \mathrm{ev}_i \,\cdot\, \mathbf{1}\!\left[\,v_i \le 2 K_c\,\right] , \]

where \(\mathrm{ev}_i\) is the patent’s expected spillover value (its group-restricted Patent-Rank social component) and the indicator restricts attention to viable projects (private value within twice the development cost). Using the global spillover value for \(\mathrm{ev}_i\) gives the global returns to R&D; using the EMDE-ex-China spillover value gives the returns within EMDE (excl. China). The gap between the two is precisely the cross-border spillover to developing economies that motivates this note’s focus on EMDE-relevant green-technology innovation.

References

Guillard, C., Martin, R., Mohnen, P., Thomas, C., & Verhoeven, D. (2021). Efficient Industrial Policy for Innovation: Standing on the Shoulders of Hidden Giants. CEP Discussion Paper No. 1813. Centre for Economic Performance, London School of Economics.

International Finance Corporation (2025). Innovation in Green Technologies. Insights Report. International Finance Corporation, World Bank Group, Washington, DC. https://www.ifc.org/en/insights-reports/2025/innovation-in-green-technologies

Kogan, L., Papanikolaou, D., Seru, A., & Stoffman, N. (2017). Technological Innovation, Resource Allocation, and Growth. The Quarterly Journal of Economics, 132(2), 665–712. https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjw040

Martin, R., & Verhoeven, D. (2023). Knowledge Spillovers from Clean Innovation: A Tradeoff between Growth and Climate? CEP Discussion Paper No. 1933. Centre for Economic Performance, London School of Economics.

CEP Special Paper No. 54. Centre for Economic Performance, London School of Economics. https://cep.lse.ac.uk/pubs/download/special/cepsp54.pdf

  1. This is derived from the methodology suggested by Guillard et al (2021). They introduce a simple model of innovation which allows us to infer the cost of innovative steps in various technology categories as well as the likely - marginal - response of innovators to lowering the cost. We provide a short summary of this approach in the appendix.↩︎
